metric_value: ATA disk up to 300 GB per disk vs 142/72/36 GB per FC/SCSI disk — 2x to 8x capacity advantage
notes: Specific capacity figures give 2-8x advantage claim its empirical basis

metric_value: ATA disks cost approximately half the price per gigabyte compared to FC/SCSI disks
notes: Half the cost per GB — fundamental economic driver for mid-line tier

metric_value: At least 20% of enterprise data fits mid-line definition per survey respondents — Aberdeen believes more than half will eventually fall into mid-line
notes: Critical market size prediction — 20% floor estimate with >50% long-term potential

metric_value: More than 60% of respondents report data restorations performed more than once a year; 20% monthly or more frequently
notes: Establishes backup/restore pain as second major use case for mid-line (alongside fixed content)

metric_value: More than 80% said when offline backups are used they could have a problem with the length of the backup window
notes: Critical pain point: backup windows too short for critical files — mid-line disk-based backup resolves this

metric_value: Nearly two-thirds of respondents said restoring critical data takes longer than they would like
notes: Mid-line disk-based restoration addresses this pain point vs tape

metric_value: About half said operational management required for backup is a burden and management concern
notes: Labor cost dimension of backup problem — mid-line reduces admin burden

metric_value: Close to half would not guarantee all critical data can be restored on a given day
notes: Risk dimension — confirms backup/restore as high-priority pain point

metric_value: 60% of respondents reported having applications with very large non-changing/static data such as video images data warehouse detail data and old email
notes: Key finding confirming fixed content as a real and widespread enterprise need

metric_value: 75% of storage buyers moderately or highly likely to buy mid-line storage in the coming year (40% highly likely; 33% moderately likely)
notes: High stated purchase intent — cautionary note added that respondents may not understand what low-cost storage really is

metric_value: 60% of respondents reported their current storage supplier had discussed low-cost storage options with them
notes: Market readiness indicator — suppliers already engaging on the topic

metric_name: Willingness to purchase higher-capacity lower-performance disks
metric_value: More than one quarter showed high willingness; nearly half showed moderate willingness; only one quarter showed little or no willingness
notes: 25% high willingness + 47% moderate willingness = 72% open to mid-line purchase

metric_value: Mid-line storage emerging in spite of storage buyer inertia with support of major disk drive manufacturers storage systems suppliers and early adopters
notes: PREDICTION: Despite conservative IT culture mid-line would be adopted driven by vendor/early-adopter support

metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]
notes: SATA-based storage became dominant in secondary storage by 2006-2008 — Phase 3 verification of timing

metric_name: Cost comparison: 2TB backup appliance SCSI vs ATA
metric_value: SCSI-based 2TB disk backup appliance: $15000-$20000; ATA-based equivalent: $7000-$10000 — roughly half price
notes: Concrete price point comparison validating the economic case

metric_value: In large FC-based disk array environments (>30 TB) saving more than $100000 off a $500000 deal still very significant even if percentage savings less dramatic
notes: Large enterprise cost savings — percentage lower but absolute value still compelling

metric_value: IT organizations need to focus on information life cycle and how content progressively moves through storage tiers as a function of value and access requirements
notes: PREDICTION: ILM thinking would become standard enterprise storage planning discipline

metric_name: ILM adoption as standard enterprise planning
metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]
notes: ILM became a dominant storage concept 2004-2008 — Phase 3 verification of depth and timeline

metric_value: Value and use of content change as content ages (birth/youth/middle age/old age); access drops dramatically as data ages
notes: First of four ILM principles defined in the white paper

metric_value: Content changes from dynamic to fixed as updates cease — becoming read-only and therefore fixed content
notes: Second ILM principle — the 'freezing' concept maps directly to the fixed-content storage category

metric_value: As data amount increases frequency of access to particular piece drops; compliance with new regulations will further reinforce this tendency
notes: Third ILM principle — regulation as accumulation driver explicitly noted

metric_value: More copies being made of content to ensure faster access and better data protection
notes: Fourth ILM principle — redundancy increases storage capacity demands

metric_value: Aberdeen believes more than half of enterprise data will eventually fall into mid-line tier
notes: PREDICTION: Long-term >50% of enterprise data in mid-line — significant long-term market sizing claim

metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]
notes: By 2010 SATA HDD and then SSD became dominant in secondary/nearline tiers — Phase 3 verification

metric_value: 75 face-to-face and telephone interviews with storage managers at companies with $1B+ revenue including several Fortune 50-size businesses; financial services over-represented; US focus
notes: Confirms survey scope and bias — financial services over-represented consistent with DiFranco survey

metric_value: Price of FC and SCSI drives has fallen dramatically but price elasticity curve economics still apply — mid-line is less costly and does backup/restore job as well
notes: Acknowledges SCSI cost decline but argues mid-line structural advantage persists

metric_value: Most data is in 'middle age' where value is less — mid-line disk is most appropriate; data in middle age is typically frozen making lower performance adequate
notes: Key insight linking ILM aging curve to mid-line product positioning

metric_name: Mid-line adding to SAN/NAS without radical change
metric_value: Mid-line can be added to existing DAS SAN and NAS products eliminating concerns about radical change in conservative IT environment
notes: PREDICTION: Integration path for mid-line would be evolutionary not revolutionary — confirmed by product history

metric_value: More than half of enterprises completely run on Fibre Channel and SCSI disks — the 'all-hammer-all-nail' problem
notes: Baseline market condition — the incumbency of FC/SCSI is the key adoption barrier for mid-line

metric_value: Mid-line storage may actually expand the market by making it more cost-effective to build a data warehouse/data mart or do data mining
notes: PREDICTION: Mid-line would expand data warehouse market by lowering cost — confirmed by NAS and SATA adoption in DW/BI

metric_name: CDP as emerging mid-line use case
metric_value: Continuous data protection (CDP) that quickly restores data to any point in time is an emerging concept enabled by mid-line disk
notes: PREDICTION: CDP would emerge as important use case — CDP did become a mainstream feature by 2005-2008

metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]
notes: Phase 3 verification — CDP adopted by NetApp EMC and others as mainstream feature by 2006-2008

metric_value: IT organizations must think about storage from a quality of service (QoS) perspective — not all applications require the same service levels
notes: Strategic recommendation: formal or informal SLAs for storage tiers

metric_value: Mid-line storage is a 'quiet' revolution — enterprises still learning best use of the new tier but widespread adoption predicted
notes: PREDICTION: Gradual but inevitable adoption — confirmed by SATA HDD trajectory
